metadata_information.title: Market Power and the Transmission of Loan Subsidies : Winners and Losers When Private Banks Distribute Government Loans : Evidence from Earmarked Credit in Brazil
metadata_information.version: Python script, from D&R API, generated on 2026-03-19

title_statement.title: Market Power and the Transmission of Loan Subsidies : Winners and Losers When Private Banks Distribute Government Loans : Evidence from Earmarked Credit in Brazil

document_description.abstract: A common strategy to improve access to credit and mitigate the effects from large financial shocks is to channel public funds to firms via private banks at regulated interest rates. Using comprehensive loan-level data from Brazil, the authors study how government loan subsidies are transmitted (or not) to targeted firms when banks exert market power. They conjecture that banks with greater market power circumvent the interest rate ceiling by increasing the price of other products to firms that receive subsidized loans. The results of this analysis have important policy implications for the design and effectiveness of government interventions in credit markets.

document_description.disclaimer: The Policy Research Working Paper Series disseminates the findings of work in progress to encourage the exchange of ideas about development issues. An objective of the series is to get the findings out quickly, even if the presentations are less than fully polished. The papers carry the names of the authors and should be cited accordingly.
